title: General purpose registers in x86-64
question: What are the general purpose registers in x86-64 and how do their sub-register names work?
answer: x86-64 provides 16 general purpose 64-bit registers: RAX, RBX, RCX, RDX, RSI, RDI, RBP, RSP, and R8 through R15. Each can be accessed at smaller widths, so RAX exposes EAX (32-bit), AX (16-bit), and AL (8-bit). Writing to a 32-bit sub-register (e.g. EAX) zero-extends into the full 64-bit register, while writing to 8/16-bit parts leaves the upper bits unchanged.
explanation: The register file grew from 8 registers in 32-bit x86 to 16 in x86-64, adding R8-R15. The zero-extension rule for 32-bit writes is a deliberate design choice that avoids partial-register stalls and makes 32-bit operations a cheap way to clear the upper half. Some registers have conventional roles: RAX is the accumulator and return value, RCX is a counter, RSP is the stack pointer, and RBP is the frame/base pointer.
codeExample:
; Sub-register views of RAX
mov rax, 0x1122334455667788
mov eax, 0xAABBCCDD   ; EAX write ZERO-EXTENDS -> RAX = 0x00000000AABBCCDD
mov ax, 0x1234        ; only low 16 bits change
mov al, 0xFF          ; only low 8 bits change

; R8-R15 need a byte suffix for narrow access
mov r8d, 42           ; 32-bit view of R8
mov r8b, 7            ; 8-bit view of R8
bestPractices: Use 32-bit writes to cheaply zero the upper 64 bits
Know the conventional role of each register before clobbering it
commonMistakes: Assuming an 8/16-bit write clears the upper bits
Forgetting RSP/RBP are general registers with special uses
interviewTips: Be able to list all 16 registers and their widths

title: Purpose of RIP, RSP and RBP
question: What are the roles of the RIP, RSP, and RBP registers?
answer: RIP is the instruction pointer, holding the address of the next instruction to execute; it cannot be written directly but changes via jumps, calls, and returns. RSP is the stack pointer, always pointing at the top of the stack (lowest used address, since the stack grows downward). RBP is the base/frame pointer, conventionally used to anchor the current stack frame so locals and arguments can be accessed at fixed offsets.
explanation: RIP-relative addressing is central to position-independent code on x86-64, letting instructions reference data by offset from RIP. RSP moves implicitly on push/pop/call/ret. RBP is optional under omit-frame-pointer optimizations, where the compiler tracks offsets from RSP instead, freeing RBP as another general register.
codeExample:
; RIP-relative addressing (position independent)
lea rax, [rip + message]   ; load address of 'message'

; RSP moves implicitly
push rax                   ; RSP -= 8, [RSP] = RAX
pop  rbx                   ; RBX = [RSP], RSP += 8

; Classic frame pointer setup
push rbp
mov  rbp, rsp              ; RBP anchors this frame
bestPractices: Use RIP-relative addressing for position-independent code
Keep RSP 16-byte aligned at call boundaries
commonMistakes: Trying to mov directly into RIP
Corrupting RSP so ret returns to the wrong address
interviewTips: Explain why the stack grows downward on x86

title: How the stack works with push and pop
question: How do push and pop manipulate the stack, and which direction does the stack grow?
answer: On x86-64 the stack grows toward lower addresses. push decrements RSP by 8 (for a 64-bit value) and stores the operand at the new top; pop reads the value at RSP and then increments RSP by 8. This makes the stack a LIFO structure used for saving registers, passing overflow arguments, and holding return addresses.
explanation: Because the stack grows down, 'top of stack' is the lowest in-use address. push/pop are effectively shorthand for adjusting RSP and doing a mov. Understanding the exact ordering matters when manually balancing the stack or reasoning about buffer layout and security (stack canaries, ROP).
codeExample:
; Save and restore registers around a call
push rbx           ; RSP -= 8
push r12           ; RSP -= 8

call some_function ; may clobber caller-saved regs

pop  r12           ; restore in REVERSE order
pop  rbx

; Equivalent of 'push rax' done manually
sub  rsp, 8
mov  [rsp], rax
bestPractices: Pop registers in reverse order of pushing
Keep push/pop balanced within a function
commonMistakes: Mismatched push/pop counts breaking ret
Assuming the stack grows upward
interviewTips: Draw the stack before and after a push

title: How call and ret work
question: What do the call and ret instructions do at the hardware level?
answer: call pushes the address of the instruction following the call (the return address) onto the stack, then transfers control to the target. ret pops that return address off the stack into RIP, resuming execution after the original call. Together they implement function invocation and return using the stack.
explanation: call is essentially 'push RIP-next; jmp target', and ret is 'pop RIP'. This is why an unbalanced stack or an overwritten return address causes ret to jump to the wrong place, the basis of stack-smashing exploits. ret can also take an immediate to pop extra bytes for callee stack cleanup in some conventions.
codeExample:
; call implicitly pushes the return address
call add_two       ; pushes address of next instr, jumps to add_two
mov  [result], rax ; execution resumes here after ret

add_two:
    add rdi, rsi
    mov rax, rdi
    ret            ; pops return address into RIP
bestPractices: Never manually overwrite the return address unless intentional
Balance the stack before ret
commonMistakes: Leaving pushed values on the stack before ret
Confusing call with an unconditional jmp
interviewTips: Describe call/ret in terms of push/pop of RIP

title: System V calling convention argument registers
question: How are function arguments passed in the System V AMD64 calling convention?
answer: Under System V AMD64 (Linux/macOS), the first six integer/pointer arguments go in RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, and R9 in that order; additional arguments are pushed on the stack. Floating point arguments use XMM0-XMM7. The integer return value comes back in RAX (and RDX for 128-bit results).
explanation: This register-based convention is faster than pushing everything to the stack. It also defines caller-saved (RAX, RCX, RDX, RSI, RDI, R8-R11) versus callee-saved (RBX, RBP, R12-R15) registers, and requires RSP to be 16-byte aligned before a call. Windows x64 uses a different order (RCX, RDX, R8, R9) with shadow space.
codeExample:
; C: long sum4(long a, long b, long c, long d)
; a=RDI, b=RSI, c=RDX, d=RCX
sum4:
    mov rax, rdi
    add rax, rsi
    add rax, rdx
    add rax, rcx   ; result in RAX
    ret

; Calling it: sum4(1, 2, 3, 4)
mov rdi, 1
mov rsi, 2
mov rdx, 3
mov rcx, 4
call sum4
bestPractices: Preserve callee-saved registers if you use them
Keep RSP 16-byte aligned at the call site
commonMistakes: Confusing System V order with Windows x64 order
Clobbering callee-saved registers without restoring
interviewTips: Memorize RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9

title: cdecl vs System V vs Windows x64
question: How does the 32-bit cdecl convention differ from 64-bit calling conventions?
answer: cdecl is a 32-bit convention where all arguments are pushed onto the stack right-to-left and the caller cleans up the stack; the return value is in EAX. In contrast, 64-bit conventions pass the first several arguments in registers: System V uses RDI/RSI/RDX/RCX/R8/R9, while Windows x64 uses RCX/RDX/R8/R9 plus 32 bytes of shadow space.
explanation: Register-based passing reduces memory traffic and is a major reason 64-bit code is often faster. cdecl's caller-cleanup allows variadic functions like printf. Knowing the ABI matters when mixing assembly with C, writing FFI bindings, or reverse engineering.
codeExample:
; 32-bit cdecl call: foo(1, 2)
push 2            ; args pushed right-to-left
push 1
call foo
add  esp, 8       ; CALLER cleans up the stack

; 64-bit System V call: foo(1, 2)
mov rdi, 1        ; args in registers
mov rsi, 2
call foo          ; no stack cleanup needed
bestPractices: Match the ABI of the code you interoperate with
Reserve shadow space when calling Windows x64 functions
commonMistakes: Forgetting caller cleanup in cdecl
Omitting shadow space on Windows x64
interviewTips: Explain why cdecl enables variadic functions

title: Memory addressing modes
question: What memory addressing modes does x86-64 support?
answer: x86-64 supports a flexible addressing form: [base + index*scale + displacement], where base and index are registers, scale is 1/2/4/8, and displacement is a constant. This single form covers direct, register-indirect, base-plus-offset, and scaled-index (array) addressing. RIP-relative addressing, [rip + disp], is also available for position-independent code.
explanation: The effective address computation happens in the addressing unit, so a single instruction like mov rax, [rbx + rcx*4 + 8] can index into an array of 4-byte elements with an offset in one step. This is why lea is often used purely for arithmetic. Understanding these modes is key to reading disassembly and optimizing array access.
codeExample:
; array[i] where array=RBX, i=RCX, element size 4
mov eax, [rbx + rcx*4]        ; scaled index

; struct field at offset 8
mov rax, [rdi + 8]            ; base + displacement

; full form: base + index*scale + disp
mov rax, [rbx + rcx*8 + 16]

; RIP-relative global
mov rax, [rip + global_var]
bestPractices: Use scaled-index addressing for array traversal
Prefer RIP-relative addressing for globals in PIC
commonMistakes: Using an unsupported scale value (only 1/2/4/8)
Using RSP as an index register (not allowed)
interviewTips: Recite the base+index*scale+disp formula

title: Difference between mov and lea
question: What is the difference between the mov and lea instructions?
answer: mov transfers the data at a memory location into a register (a dereference), whereas lea (Load Effective Address) computes the address expression and stores the address itself, without touching memory. Because lea evaluates base+index*scale+displacement, it doubles as a fast arithmetic instruction.
explanation: mov rax, [rbx+8] loads the 8 bytes stored at rbx+8; lea rax, [rbx+8] just sets rax = rbx+8. Compilers frequently exploit lea to do multiplications and additions in one instruction without affecting the FLAGS register, which is why you see lea used for math that has nothing to do with addresses.
codeExample:
mov rbx, 0x1000

mov rax, [rbx + 8]   ; RAX = *(rbx+8): reads memory
lea rax, [rbx + 8]   ; RAX = rbx+8 = 0x1008: no memory access

; lea as arithmetic: rax = rdi*4 + rsi + 1
lea rax, [rsi + rdi*4 + 1]  ; does NOT change FLAGS
bestPractices: Use lea for combined multiply-add without setting flags
Use mov when you actually need the value in memory
commonMistakes: Thinking lea reads memory
Expecting lea to update FLAGS like add
interviewTips: Show a lea used purely as arithmetic

title: Arithmetic and logic instructions
question: What are the core arithmetic and logic instructions in x86-64 and how do they affect flags?
answer: Common arithmetic instructions include add, sub, imul/mul, idiv/div, inc, and dec; logic instructions include and, or, xor, and not. Most of these (except inc/dec's effect on CF, and mov/lea) update the FLAGS register, setting ZF, SF, CF, and OF based on the result, which subsequent conditional jumps read.
explanation: Signed and unsigned variants matter: imul/idiv are signed, mul/div are unsigned, and division uses RDX:RAX as the dividend so you must sign-extend (cqo) or zero RDX first. xor reg, reg is the idiomatic way to zero a register because it is short and breaks dependency chains.
codeExample:
xor eax, eax        ; idiomatic zero of RAX
add rax, rbx        ; RAX += RBX, sets flags
sub rax, 1          ; RAX -= 1
imul rax, rbx       ; signed multiply

; signed division: RAX / RCX
mov rax, 100
mov rcx, 7
cqo                 ; sign-extend RAX into RDX:RAX
idiv rcx            ; quotient->RAX, remainder->RDX
bestPractices: Zero or sign-extend RDX before div/idiv
Use xor reg,reg to zero registers
commonMistakes: Forgetting to set RDX before division
Mixing signed and unsigned mul/div
interviewTips: Explain the RDX:RAX dividend requirement

title: Function prologue and epilogue
question: What is a function prologue and epilogue, and why are they used?
answer: The prologue is code at the start of a function that sets up the stack frame: it typically pushes RBP, copies RSP into RBP, and subtracts from RSP to reserve space for local variables. The epilogue reverses this before returning, restoring RSP and RBP (often via leave) and then executing ret.
explanation: Establishing a frame pointer gives stable offsets for locals and arguments and enables debuggers to unwind the stack. The leave instruction is shorthand for mov rsp, rbp; pop rbp. With frame-pointer-omission optimizations the prologue may skip RBP entirely and reference everything relative to RSP.
codeExample:
my_func:
    ; --- prologue ---
    push rbp
    mov  rbp, rsp
    sub  rsp, 32        ; reserve 32 bytes for locals

    mov  dword [rbp-4], 10   ; a local variable

    ; --- epilogue ---
    mov  rsp, rbp       ; or use 'leave'
    pop  rbp
    ret
bestPractices: Reserve local space in one sub to keep alignment
Use leave to simplify the epilogue
commonMistakes: Forgetting to restore RSP causing a corrupt ret
Reserving space that misaligns the stack
interviewTips: Explain what the leave instruction expands to

title: Stack frames and local variables
question: How are local variables stored and accessed within a stack frame?
answer: Local variables live in the space reserved on the stack by the prologue (via sub rsp, N). They are accessed at fixed negative offsets from RBP (e.g. [rbp-4], [rbp-8]) or at positive offsets from RSP when no frame pointer is used. Function arguments passed on the stack sit at positive offsets from RBP.
explanation: Because locals are just stack memory, their lifetime ends when the frame is torn down. Layout and alignment matter for performance and correctness (e.g. SIMD types needing 16-byte alignment). Buffer overflows in these locals are what stack canaries and non-executable stacks defend against.
codeExample:
; int f(int arg) { int local = arg * 2; return local; }
f:
    push rbp
    mov  rbp, rsp
    sub  rsp, 16
    mov  [rbp-4], edi      ; store arg into a local slot
    mov  eax, [rbp-4]
    add  eax, eax          ; local = arg * 2
    mov  [rbp-8], eax      ; another local
    leave
    ret
bestPractices: Group locals to satisfy alignment requirements
Keep track of RBP-relative offsets carefully
commonMistakes: Reading a local outside its frame's lifetime
Overlapping offsets for different variables
interviewTips: Distinguish RBP-relative locals from RBP-positive arguments

title: Immediate, register, and memory operands
question: What is the difference between immediate, register, and memory operands?
answer: An immediate operand is a constant encoded directly in the instruction (e.g. mov rax, 5). A register operand names a CPU register (mov rax, rbx). A memory operand references data in RAM through an address expression in brackets (mov rax, [rbx]). x86 does not allow two memory operands in a single instruction.
explanation: Operand type affects encoding size and speed: immediates and registers are fastest, memory operands require an address calculation and a bus/cache access. The single-memory-operand restriction means memory-to-memory moves need an intermediate register, a frequent source of extra instructions.
codeExample:
mov rax, 42        ; immediate operand
mov rax, rbx       ; register operand
mov rax, [rbx]     ; memory operand (load)
mov [rcx], rax     ; memory operand (store)

; ILLEGAL: two memory operands
; mov [rcx], [rbx]
; must go through a register:
mov rax, [rbx]
mov [rcx], rax
bestPractices: Use a register to bridge memory-to-memory transfers
Prefer immediates/registers in hot loops
commonMistakes: Writing an illegal memory-to-memory move
Forgetting a size specifier on an immediate-to-memory store
interviewTips: State that x86 forbids two memory operands

title: Endianness on x86
question: What is endianness and how does it apply to x86-64?
answer: Endianness describes the byte order used to store multi-byte values in memory. x86-64 is little-endian, meaning the least significant byte is stored at the lowest address. So the 32-bit value 0x12345678 is stored in memory as bytes 78 56 34 12.
explanation: Little-endian ordering affects how you interpret memory dumps, parse binary formats, and exchange data over networks (which use big-endian, 'network byte order'). Instructions like bswap or movbe convert between orderings. ARM can operate in either mode but is typically little-endian too.
codeExample:
; store 0x12345678 at address in RBX
mov dword [rbx], 0x12345678
; memory bytes (low->high address): 78 56 34 12

; reading a single byte gets the LSB first
movzx eax, byte [rbx]   ; EAX = 0x78

; convert to big-endian (network order)
mov eax, 0x12345678
bswap eax               ; EAX = 0x78563412
bestPractices: Use bswap/movbe when parsing big-endian formats
Document byte order when serializing binary data
commonMistakes: Assuming memory bytes appear in written order
Ignoring endianness in cross-platform binary I/O
interviewTips: Give the byte layout of a sample hex value

title: cmp, jmp and conditional jumps
question: How do cmp and conditional jump instructions like je/jne work together?
answer: cmp subtracts its second operand from the first without storing the result, updating the FLAGS register. Conditional jumps then branch based on those flags: je (ZF=1) jumps if equal, jne (ZF=0) if not equal, jg/jl for signed comparisons, and ja/jb for unsigned. jmp is an unconditional branch.
explanation: cmp is essentially a sub that only sets flags. The distinction between signed (jg, jl, jge, jle) and unsigned (ja, jb, jae, jbe) jumps is critical, using the wrong family after comparing signed values causes subtle bugs. test (a non-destructive and) is commonly paired with jz/jnz for bit checks.
codeExample:
cmp rax, rbx     ; sets flags from (rax - rbx)
je  equal        ; jump if rax == rbx
jg  greater      ; signed: jump if rax > rbx
jb  below        ; unsigned: jump if rax < rbx

; test for zero without cmp
test rax, rax
jz   is_zero     ; jump if rax == 0
bestPractices: Match signed jumps (jg/jl) to signed data and unsigned (ja/jb) to unsigned
Use test reg,reg to check for zero
commonMistakes: Using unsigned jumps after a signed comparison
Assuming cmp stores its result
interviewTips: Contrast jg/jl with ja/jb

title: The FLAGS register
question: What is the FLAGS (RFLAGS) register and which flags matter most?
answer: RFLAGS is a special register holding status bits set by arithmetic and logic operations. The key status flags are ZF (zero), SF (sign), CF (carry, for unsigned overflow), and OF (signed overflow); PF (parity) and AF (auxiliary) also exist. Conditional instructions read these flags to decide behavior.
explanation: CF and OF distinguish unsigned versus signed overflow, which is why there are separate jump families. Flags persist until the next flag-affecting instruction, so you can compute once and branch multiple times. Instructions like lea and mov deliberately do not modify flags, which is useful for reordering.
codeExample:
mov al, 250
add al, 10        ; result wraps to 4
                  ; CF=1 (unsigned overflow), ZF=0, SF=0

mov al, 127
add al, 1         ; result = -128 as signed
                  ; OF=1 (signed overflow), SF=1

sub rax, rax      ; ZF=1 (result is zero)
jz  handle_zero
bestPractices: Choose CF-based jumps for unsigned and OF-based for signed logic
Remember lea/mov leave flags untouched
commonMistakes: Confusing CF (unsigned) with OF (signed) overflow
Assuming every instruction updates flags
interviewTips: Explain CF vs OF with a wrap-around example

title: Writing loops in assembly
question: How do you implement a loop in x86-64 assembly?
answer: Loops are built from a label, a body, an update, and a conditional jump back to the label. A common pattern uses a counter register, decrements it, and uses cmp/jne or dec/jnz to continue. The dedicated loop instruction decrements RCX and jumps if non-zero, but explicit dec + jnz is usually faster on modern CPUs.
explanation: Because there is no high-level for construct, you manually manage initialization, the continuation test, and the increment. Placing the test at the bottom (a do-while shape) avoids an extra jump per iteration. The loop instruction is compact but discouraged for performance-critical code.
codeExample:
; sum 0..n-1 into RAX, n in RDI
    xor rax, rax        ; sum = 0
    xor rcx, rcx        ; i = 0
.loop:
    cmp rcx, rdi
    jge .done           ; exit when i >= n
    add rax, rcx        ; sum += i
    inc rcx             ; i++
    jmp .loop
.done:
    ret
bestPractices: Prefer dec/jnz over the loop instruction
Put the exit test to minimize branches per iteration
commonMistakes: Forgetting to update the counter (infinite loop)
Using a signed jump for an unsigned counter
interviewTips: Show a bottom-tested loop for efficiency

title: Bitwise operations and shifts
question: What bitwise and shift instructions does x86-64 provide, and what are they used for?
answer: Bitwise instructions include and, or, xor, and not for masking, setting, toggling, and clearing bits. Shift instructions include shl/sal (left), shr (logical right), and sar (arithmetic right), plus rotates rol/ror. Shifts by powers of two are a fast substitute for multiplication and division.
explanation: shr fills with zeros while sar replicates the sign bit, so sar preserves signedness when dividing negative numbers. and with a mask isolates bit fields, or sets bits, and xor toggles them (and xor reg,reg clears a register). These operations are foundational for flags, permissions, hashing, and hardware register manipulation in embedded code.
codeExample:
mov eax, 0b10110100
and eax, 0x0F      ; mask low nibble -> 0b0100
or  eax, 0x80      ; set high bit
xor eax, 0xFF      ; toggle low 8 bits

shl eax, 3         ; eax *= 8 (unsigned)
sar eax, 1         ; signed divide by 2
shr eax, 1         ; unsigned divide by 2
bestPractices: Use sar for signed and shr for unsigned right shifts
Use shifts instead of mul/div by powers of two
commonMistakes: Using shr on signed values, corrupting the sign
Confusing logical and arithmetic shifts
interviewTips: Explain the difference between sar and shr

title: Text, data, and bss sections
question: What are the .text, .data, and .bss sections in an assembly program?
answer: The .text section holds executable machine code and is typically read-only. The .data section holds initialized global and static variables that occupy space in the binary. The .bss section holds uninitialized (zero-initialized) globals; it reserves space but stores no bytes in the file, so the loader zeros it at startup.
explanation: Separating sections lets the OS apply different memory protections: .text is executable but not writable (defending against code injection), .data is writable, and .bss saves file size by not storing zeros. There is also .rodata for read-only constants like string literals.
codeExample:
section .data
msg:    db "Hello", 0      ; initialized bytes
count:  dq 42

section .bss
buffer: resb 256           ; 256 uninitialized bytes

section .text
global _start
_start:
    mov rax, [count]
    ; ...
bestPractices: Put large zero-initialized buffers in .bss to shrink the binary
Keep constants in .rodata for safety
commonMistakes: Putting mutable data in .text
Expecting .bss contents in the file image
interviewTips: Explain why .bss reduces binary size

title: Interrupts and system calls
question: How do interrupts and system calls invoke the operating system from assembly?
answer: A system call requests a service from the kernel. On 64-bit Linux you place the syscall number in RAX, arguments in RDI, RSI, RDX, R10, R8, R9, then execute the syscall instruction; the result returns in RAX. Older 32-bit code used the software interrupt int 0x80. Hardware interrupts, by contrast, are asynchronous signals from devices handled via the interrupt descriptor table.
explanation: The syscall instruction is faster than int 0x80 because it avoids full interrupt-gate overhead. Note the syscall convention uses R10 instead of RCX for the fourth argument, since syscall clobbers RCX and R11. Understanding this is essential for writing freestanding programs without libc.
codeExample:
; Linux x86-64: write(1, msg, len) then exit(0)
section .text
global _start
_start:
    mov rax, 1          ; syscall: write
    mov rdi, 1          ; fd = stdout
    lea rsi, [rel msg]  ; buffer
    mov rdx, 5          ; length
    syscall

    mov rax, 60         ; syscall: exit
    xor rdi, rdi        ; status 0
    syscall
section .data
msg: db "Hello", 10
bestPractices: Use R10 for the 4th syscall argument, not RCX
Check RAX for negative error codes after syscall
commonMistakes: Reusing RCX for arg4 in a syscall
Confusing the syscall ABI with the function-call ABI
interviewTips: Contrast syscall with int 0x80

title: AT&T vs Intel syntax and assemblers
question: What is the difference between AT&T and Intel syntax, and how do NASM and GAS relate?
answer: Intel and AT&T are two syntaxes for the same x86 instructions. Intel syntax (used by NASM and MASM) writes destination first: mov rax, 5. AT&T syntax (default in GAS/GCC) reverses operands, prefixes registers with % and immediates with $, and appends size suffixes: movq $5, %rax. NASM is a standalone Intel-syntax assembler; GAS is the GNU assembler that defaults to AT&T but supports Intel via a directive.
explanation: Beyond operand order, AT&T uses different memory syntax: disp(base, index, scale) versus Intel's [base + index*scale + disp]. Choosing a syntax is often dictated by the toolchain, disassembler output, or existing codebase. Both produce identical machine code.
codeExample:
; Intel syntax (NASM): dest, src
mov  rax, 5
add  rax, rbx
mov  rcx, [rbx + rdi*4 + 8]

; AT&T syntax (GAS): src, dest with prefixes
movq  $5, %rax
addq  %rbx, %rax
movq  8(%rbx, %rdi, 4), %rcx

; GAS can switch to Intel:
.intel_syntax noprefix
bestPractices: Match the syntax expected by your toolchain
Use .intel_syntax noprefix in GAS if you prefer Intel
commonMistakes: Reversing operand order between syntaxes
Forgetting AT&T's %/$ prefixes and size suffixes
interviewTips: Show the same instruction in both syntaxes
